metadata.description: Acute elevation of intracompartmental pressure threatening limb perfusion.
Four-compartment osteofascial model of the lower leg.
Anterior leg compartment containing dorsiflexor musculature.
Lateral leg compartment containing evertor musculature.
Deep posterior leg compartment containing flexor hallucis longus and tibialis posterior.
Superficial posterior leg compartment containing gastrocnemius and soleus.
Emergent surgical release of leg compartments to restore perfusion.

proposals.evidence_summary: Pilot entity: Pain with Passive Stretch

metadata.description: Early clinical sign of compartment ischemia on passive muscle stretch.
Delta pressure framework relating diastolic pressure to compartment pressure.
High-risk antecedent injury frequently associated with leg compartment syndrome.
Irreversible muscle injury from prolonged compartment ischemia.
Ischemic contracture after untreated compartment syndrome.
Muscle breakdown with systemic metabolic consequences after ischemia.
Catastrophic outcome after delayed fasciotomy and irreversible ischemia.

metadata.claim_text: Compartment syndrome is a clinical diagnosis where escalating pain, tense compartments, and pain with passive stretch outweigh waiting for confirmatory imaging.
Pulselessness is a late finding — do not exclude compartment syndrome when distal pulses are still present.
Pain with passive stretch of involved musculature is an earlier and more useful sign than absent pulses.
Leg compartment syndrome requires understanding all four osteofascial compartments — anterior, lateral, deep posterior, and superficial posterior.
Delaying fasciotomy to obtain imaging or pressure measurements when the clinical picture is evolving.
Compartment pressure measurements are adjuncts only — they should not delay treatment when clinical suspicion is high.
On consult, state time-sensitive limb emergency, repeat compartment exam, loosen dressings, and escalate for emergent fasciotomy.

metadata.trigger: Escalating pain out of proportion, tense compartments, pain with passive stretch, or evolving neurovascular deficit
metadata.action: Proceed with emergent leg fasciotomy — do not delay for imaging when clinical suspicion is high

metadata.trigger: Equivocal exam in obtunded patient or unreliable exam conditions
metadata.action: Measure compartment pressures as adjunct; maintain low threshold for fasciotomy if trending abnormal
